questions.question: What datasets did 'An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale' use?
questions.gold_answer: ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, CIFAR-100
questions.system_answer: The paper reports pre-training on ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, and JFT-300M. It also evaluates transfer performance on smaller benchmarks including ImageNet, ImageNet-Real, CIFAR-100, and the VTAB suite of 19 tasks.

questions.question: What is the parameter count of the largest variant in 'Training data-efficient image transformers & distillation through attention'?
questions.gold_answer: The largest DeiT variant is DeiT-B with 86M parameters. Note: ViT-L (307M) appears in the paper as a comparison baseline, not as a DeiT model variant.
questions.system_answer: The largest variant listed in the evidence is ViT-L, with 307M parameters. If you mean the largest DeiT variant in this paper, DeiT-B★ is 87M parameters.

questions.question: What architectural innovations does 'Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision Transformer using Shifted Windows' introduce?
questions.gold_answer: hierarchical structure, shifted window attention
questions.system_answer: The paper introduces a hierarchical vision Transformer that computes self-attention within non-overlapping local windows, then alternates with shifted window partitioning to create cross-window connections. It also uses patch merging to build multi-scale feature maps, yielding linear computational complexity with respect to image size; the evidence also notes relative position bias as an architectural choice that improves performance.

questions.question: Briefly describe the architecture of 'Emerging Properties in Self-Supervised Vision Transformers'.
questions.gold_answer: DINO is a self-supervised vision transformer training method
questions.system_answer: The paper presents DINO, a simple self-supervised Vision Transformer architecture based on self-distillation without labels. A student network is trained to match a momentum-teacher network across multiple augmented views, using centering and sharpening to prevent collapse; it is designed to work with both ViTs and convnets, though the paper highlights strong gains for ViTs with small patches.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on top-1 accuracy on ImageNet?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported top-1 ImageNet accuracy in this corpus varies widely from 1.3% to 91.0% across 594 results.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus shows a very wide spread in reported ImageNet top-1 accuracy, from 1.3 to 91.0 (stddev 8.69), so the results are not consistent. There are also conflicting SOTA claims across papers, with different models/papers reporting top values such as 91.0 for CoCa (finetuned), 90.9 for PaLI-17B, 90.6 for CoCa (frozen), and 88.7/88.51 for MaxViT-XL.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on which model holds SOTA on ADE20K mIoU?
questions.gold_answer: No—this corpus does not agree on a single ADE20K mIoU SOTA model, since 7 papers each claim SOTA on the benchmark.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus does not fully agree: the highest ADE20K mIoU in the evidence is 62.9 from InternImage-H, and that paper is the strongest SOTA claim listed. However, another paper, ViT-Adapter-L, also claims SOTA at 61.5, so there are conflicting SOTA claims even though InternImage-H has the top reported value in this corpus.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on mIoU values on ADE20K?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported ADE20K mIoU values vary widely across 97 results, from 26.6 to 62.9.
questions.system_answer: No. The reported ADE20K mIoU values vary widely across papers, from 26.6 to 62.9 (spread 36.3, stddev 7.29), so they are not consistent. There are also conflicting SOTA claims: InternImage-H is listed at 62.9, while ViT-Adapter-L is listed at 61.5 and SwinV2-G at 59.9.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on whether position embeddings are necessary for vision transformers?
questions.gold_answer: Papers disagree on the necessity of position embeddings; some ablations show minimal impact while others demonstrate clear performance drops without them.
questions.system_answer: The corpus does not fully agree. Several papers treat positional information as needed for vision transformers: one says self-attention is permutation-equivariant and that “positional encodings are commonly employed” and another states that vanilla Transformers “utilize[] the positional encoding added in the input embeddings” to model sequence information. But one vision-transformer paper argues the opposite direction for its setting, saying “the positional encodings used here have two issues” and proposing a method to provide position information instead, which suggests position embeddings are useful but not necessarily in their standard form. There is no direct contradiction that position information matters; the disagreement is about whether the usual absolute/relative embeddings are necessary or the best way to supply it.

questions.question: How did the maximum reported top-1 accuracy on ImageNet change year over year in this corpus?
questions.system_answer: The maximum reported top-1 ImageNet accuracy rose from 85.2 in 2020 to 90.0 in 2021 and 91.0 in 2022, then declined to 87.8 in 2023 and 83.9 in 2024. Overall, the trend is up from 2020 to a peak in 2022, followed by a downward trend through 2024.
